questions.questionText: Tc-99m pertechnetate scan is used to detect ectopic gastric mucosa in:
questions.optionB: Meckel's diverticulum (ectopic gastric mucosa takes up Tc-99m pertechnetate — sensitivity ~85% in children for bleeding Meckel's)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Meckel's scan uses Tc-99m pertechnetate which is taken up by gastric mucosa (including ectopic). Why correct: Meckel's diverticulum (remnant of vitellointestinal duct) contains ectopic gastric mucosa in 50% → Tc-99m uptake in RLQ. Rule of 2's: 2% population, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of tissue (gastric/pancreatic). Why A wrong: Crohn's — diagnosed by colonoscopy/CT enterography. Why C wrong: Appendicitis — USG/CT. Why D wrong: Intussusception — USG/air enema. Clinical relevance: Most common congenital GI anomaly. Presents with painless GI bleeding in children. Pretreat with pentagastrin/H2 blockers to enhance sensitivity. Exam trap: Meckel's scan only detects diverticula with GASTRIC mucosa — 50% have it. False negative = no gastric mucosa. Memory aid: 'Meckel's = Technetium detects gastric Mucosa in Misplaced diverticulum.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Nuclear Medicine.

questions.questionText: PET-CT using F-18 FDG is based on the principle that malignant cells have:
questions.optionB: Increased glucose metabolism (Warburg effect — cancer cells preferentially use aerobic glycolysis → high FDG uptake)
questions.explanation: Core concept: FDG-PET exploits the Warburg effect — cancer cells preferentially take up glucose. Why correct: F-18 FDG = glucose analog. Cancer cells have upregulated glucose transporters (GLUT-1) + hexokinase → trap FDG intracellularly → 'hot spot' on PET. SUV (Standardized Uptake Value) quantifies uptake. Why A wrong: Malignant cells have INCREASED metabolic activity. Why C wrong: FDG traces glucose, not fat metabolism. Why D wrong: Tumors often have increased vascularity. Clinical relevance: Uses: staging, restaging, treatment response assessment, unknown primary. False positives: infection, inflammation, granulomas. False negatives: low-grade tumors (bronchioloalveolar carcinoma, mucinous tumors). Exam trap: PET-CT cannot differentiate infection from malignancy reliably — both are FDG-avid. Memory aid: 'FDG-PET = cancer cells eat more sugar (Warburg effect).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Nuclear Medicine.

questions.questionText: Bone scintigraphy (Tc-99m MDP bone scan) may be FALSE NEGATIVE in:
questions.optionB: Multiple myeloma (purely lytic lesion with no osteoblastic response — bone scan detects osteoblastic activity, not lysis)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Bone scan detects osteoblastic activity — purely lytic lesions like myeloma may be missed. Why correct: Tc-99m MDP binds to hydroxyapatite in areas of increased bone turnover/osteoblastic activity. Myeloma is purely lytic (no osteoblastic response) → cold/normal on bone scan. Use skeletal survey or PET-CT instead. Why A wrong: Osteoblastic mets (prostate) → intensely hot on bone scan. Why C wrong: Paget's → very hot (high turnover). Why D wrong: Stress fractures → hot (healing response). Clinical relevance: Bone scan sensitivity >95% for osteoblastic mets. Super scan: diffusely increased skeletal uptake with faint kidney visualization = widespread mets. Exam trap: Myeloma + renal cell + thyroid mets = may be cold/false negative on bone scan. These are LYTIC predominant. Memory aid: 'Bone scan = Blastic activity. Myeloma = Missing (no blastic reaction).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Nuclear Medicine.
questions.conceptTag: Bone scan false negative myeloma lytic

questions.questionText: Radioactive iodine (I-131) therapy is used for treatment of:
questions.optionB: Differentiated thyroid carcinoma (papillary and follicular) and Graves' disease
questions.explanation: Core concept: I-131 is taken up by differentiated thyroid tissue (papillary/follicular) for ablation. Why correct: Differentiated thyroid carcinoma retains ability to trap iodine → I-131 ablation after total thyroidectomy. Also used for Graves' disease (hyperthyroid ablation). Why A wrong: MTC arises from C-cells (parafollicular) — does NOT take up iodine. Treatment: surgery. Why C wrong: Anaplastic — undifferentiated, does NOT concentrate iodine. Poor prognosis. Why D wrong: Lymphoma — treated with chemotherapy/radiation. Clinical relevance: Post-ablation whole-body scan with I-131 to detect residual/metastatic disease. Thyroglobulin: tumor marker for follow-up. Exam trap: Only DIFFERENTIATED (papillary + follicular) take up iodine. Medullary + anaplastic do NOT. Memory aid: 'I-131 = Iodine for Differentiated tumors only (D for Differentiated).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Nuclear Medicine.
